mn27:0.2: The Shorter Simile of the Elephant’s Footprint
mn27:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn27:10.1: “Suppose, brahmin, an elephant tracker were to enter an elephant forest.
mn27:10.10: ‘Surely, sir, this is a great bull elephant.’
mn27:10.12: Because, brahmin, there are in the elephant forest tall she-elephants called kāḷārikās, with large feet, and this might be their footprint.
mn27:10.14: Following it, he sees in the elephant forest a large elephant footprint, long and broad, the marks of a high reach, and high places gouged by tusks.
mn27:10.15: But a skilled elephant tracker does not yet come to a conclusion:
mn27:10.16: ‘Surely, sir, this is a great bull elephant.’
mn27:10.18: Because, brahmin, there are in the elephant forest tall she-elephants called kaṇerukās, with large feet, and this might be their footprint.
mn27:10.2: There he might see a large elephant footprint, long and broad.
mn27:10.20: Following it, he sees in the elephant forest a large elephant footprint, long and broad, the marks of a high reach, high places gouged by tusks, and high places where branches have been broken.
mn27:10.21: And he sees that bull elephant at the root of a tree, or in an open place, walking, standing, sitting, or lying down.
mn27:10.22: Then he comes to a conclusion:
mn27:10.23: ‘This indeed is that great bull elephant.’
mn27:10.3: But a skilled elephant tracker does not yet come to a conclusion:
mn27:10.4: ‘Surely, sir, this is a great bull elephant.’
mn27:10.6: Because, brahmin, there are in the elephant forest she-elephants called vāmanikās, with large feet, and this might be their footprint.
mn27:10.8: Following it, he sees in the elephant forest a large elephant footprint, long and broad, and the marks of a high reach.
mn27:10.9: But a skilled elephant tracker does not yet come to a conclusion:
mn27:11.1: Just so, brahmin, a Tathāgata arises in the world: an arahant, perfectly awakened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, fortunate, knower of the world, unsurpassed guide of persons to be tamed, teacher of devas and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.
mn27:11.2: Having realized for himself with direct knowledge this world with its devas, its Māras, its Brahmās, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its devas and humans, he makes it known.
mn27:11.3: He teaches the *dhamma* that is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-worded, and he reveals a spiritual life that is wholly complete and pure.
mn27:12.1: A householder, or a householder’s son, or someone born in another family, hears that *dhamma*.
mn27:12.2: Having heard that *dhamma*, he gains faith in the Tathāgata.
mn27:12.3: Endowed with that gain of faith, he reflects thus:
mn27:12.4: ‘The household life is crowded, a dusty road; going forth is wide open.
mn27:12.5: It is not easy, while living in a house, to live the spiritual life in all its fullness and purity, polished like a shell.
mn27:12.6: What if I were to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from home into homelessness?’
mn27:12.7: After some time, having abandoned a small mass of wealth or a great mass of wealth, having abandoned a small circle of relatives or a great circle of relatives, he shaves off hair and beard, puts on ochre robes, and goes forth from home into homelessness.
mn27:13.1: Having thus gone forth, he undertakes the training and way of life of *bhikkhus*. Abandoning the killing of living beings, he refrains from the killing of living beings. With rod laid down and weapon laid down, conscientious, compassionate, he dwells caring for the welfare of all living beings.
mn27:13.10: he refrains from dancing, singing, music, and shows,
mn27:13.11: he refrains from garlands, perfumes, ointments, adornment, and beautification,
mn27:13.12: he refrains from high and luxurious beds,
mn27:13.13: he refrains from accepting gold and silver,
mn27:13.14: he refrains from accepting raw grain,
mn27:13.15: he refrains from accepting raw meat,
mn27:13.16: he refrains from accepting women and girls,
mn27:13.17: he refrains from accepting female and male slaves,
mn27:13.18: he refrains from accepting goats and sheep,
mn27:13.19: he refrains from accepting fowl and pigs,
mn27:13.2: Abandoning the taking of what is not given, he refrains from taking what is not given; he takes only what is given, expecting only what is given. He dwells pure in himself, not stealing.
mn27:13.20: he refrains from accepting elephants, cattle, horses, and mares,
mn27:13.21: he refrains from accepting fields and land,
mn27:13.22: he refrains from engaging in errands and messenger work,
mn27:13.23: he refrains from buying and selling,
mn27:13.24: he refrains from false weights, false metals, and false measures,
mn27:13.25: he refrains from fraud, deception, trickery, and misrepresentation,
mn27:13.26: he refrains from cutting, killing, binding, robbery, plundering, and violence.
mn27:13.3: Abandoning the non-spiritual life, he lives the spiritual life, living apart, refraining from sexual intercourse, the village practice.
mn27:13.4: Abandoning false speech, he refrains from false speech. He speaks truth, is devoted to truth, reliable, trustworthy, not deceptive to the world.
mn27:13.5: Abandoning divisive speech, he refrains from divisive speech. What he has heard here he does not repeat elsewhere in order to divide these people from those; and what he has heard elsewhere he does not repeat here in order to divide those people from these. Thus he is one who reunites those who are divided and supports those who are united; he delights in concord, rejoices in concord, enjoys concord, and speaks words that create concord.
mn27:13.6: Abandoning harsh speech, he refrains from harsh speech. He speaks words that are gentle, pleasing to the ear, affectionate, going to the heart, courteous, desired by many, agreeable to many.
mn27:13.7: Abandoning idle chatter, he refrains from idle chatter. He speaks at the proper time, speaks what is factual, what is beneficial, speaks on *dhamma*, speaks on *vinaya*; he speaks words worth treasuring, timely, reasoned, measured, and connected with the benefit.
mn27:13.8: He refrains from damaging seeds and plants,
mn27:13.9: he is one who eats once a day, refraining at night, refraining from eating at improper times,
mn27:14.1: He is content with robes to protect the body and with almsfood to maintain the belly. Wherever he goes, he goes carrying just these along.
mn27:14.2: Just as a bird, wherever it flies, flies with its wings as its only burden,
mn27:14.3: so too a *bhikkhu* is content with robes to protect the body and with almsfood to maintain the belly. Wherever he goes, he goes carrying just these along.
mn27:14.4: Endowed with this noble aggregate of ethical conduct, he experiences within himself a blameless *sukha*.
mn27:15.1: Having seen a form with the eye, he does not grasp at the *nimitta* or the particulars.
mn27:15.2: Since, if he were to dwell with the eye-*indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*] unguarded, bad unskillful *dhammas* of covetousness and distress might invade him, he practices for its restraint, he guards the eye-*indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], he undertakes restraint regarding the eye-*indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*].
mn27:15.3: Having heard a sound with the ear …
mn27:15.4: having smelled an odor with the nose …
mn27:15.5: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
mn27:15.6: having felt a tangible thing with the body …
mn27:15.7: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, he does not grasp at the *nimitta* or the particulars.
mn27:15.8: Since, if he were to dwell with the mind-*indriya* [*manindriyaṁ*] unguarded, bad unskillful *dhammas* of covetousness and distress might invade him, he practices for its restraint, he guards the mind-*indriya* [*manindriyaṁ*], he undertakes restraint regarding the mind-*indriya* [*manindriyaṁ*].
mn27:15.9: Endowed with this noble restraint of the *indriyas*, he experiences within himself an unstained *sukha*.
mn27:16.1: He acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when going forward and returning; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when looking ahead and looking away; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when bending and stretching; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when wearing his outer robe and carrying his bowl and robes; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when defecating and urinating; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when walking, standing, sitting, falling asleep, waking up, speaking, and keeping silent.
mn27:17.1: Endowed with this noble aggregate of ethical conduct, and endowed with this noble contentment, endowed with this noble restraint of the *indriyas*, and endowed with this noble *sati* and full awareness [*satisampajaññena*],
mn27:17.2: he resorts to a secluded resting place: a forest, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a hillside cave, a charnel ground, a jungle grove, an open space, or a heap of straw.
mn27:18.1: After returning from the alms round, after the meal, he sits down, folding his legs crosswise, setting his body erect, and establishing *sati* before him.
mn27:18.2: Abandoning covetousness for the world, he dwells with a mind free of covetousness; he purifies the mind [*citta*] of covetousness.
mn27:18.3: Abandoning ill will and hatred, he dwells with a mind free of ill will [*abyāpannacitto*], compassionate for the welfare of all living beings; he purifies the mind [*citta*] of ill will and hatred.
mn27:18.4: Abandoning dullness and drowsiness, he dwells free from dullness and drowsiness, perceiving light, with *sati*, fully aware [*sampajāno*]; he purifies the mind [*citta*] of dullness and drowsiness.
mn27:18.5: Abandoning restlessness and remorse, he dwells unagitated, with a mind inwardly stilled [*vūpasantacitto*]; he purifies the mind [*citta*] of restlessness and remorse.
mn27:18.6: Abandoning doubt, he dwells having crossed beyond doubt, free from perplexity regarding skillful *dhammas*; he purifies the mind [*citta*] of doubt.
mn27:19.1: Having abandoned these five hindrances, impurities of the mind, weakeners of discernment [*paññāya*],
mn27:19.2: quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which is accompanied by thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn27:19.3: This too is called, brahmin, a footprint of the Tathāgata, and also something frequented by the Tathāgata, and also something marked by the Tathāgata.
mn27:19.4: But the noble disciple does not yet come to a conclusion:
mn27:19.5: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:2.1: Now at that time Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin was leaving Sāvatthī in an all-white mare-drawn chariot in the middle of the day.
mn27:2.10: “Who am I, sir, to praise the ascetic Gotama?
mn27:2.11: Master Gotama is praised by those praised as the best among devas and humans.”
mn27:2.12: “But seeing what benefit is Master Vacchāyana so devoted to the ascetic Gotama?”
mn27:2.2: Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin saw the wanderer Pilotika coming from afar.
mn27:2.3: Seeing the wanderer Pilotika, he said to him:
mn27:2.4: “Come now, where is Master Vacchāyana coming from in the middle of the day?”
mn27:2.5: “Just now, sir, I am coming from the presence of the ascetic Gotama.”
mn27:2.6: “What does Master Vacchāyana think of the ascetic Gotama’s skill in discernment [*paññāveyyattiyaṁ*]? I take it he is wise.”
mn27:2.7: “Who am I, sir, and what am I, that I should know the ascetic Gotama’s skill in discernment [*paññāveyyattiyaṁ*]?
mn27:2.8: Surely one would have to be just like him to know the ascetic Gotama’s skill in discernment [*paññāveyyattiyaṁ*].”
mn27:2.9: “Indeed, Master Vacchāyana praises the ascetic Gotama with lofty praise.”
mn27:20.1: Again, brahmin, with the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with inner confidence, with the mind’s unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*], without thought [*avitakkaṁ*] and without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijaṁ*], the *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*.
mn27:20.3: ‘the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:21.1: Again, brahmin, with the fading away of *pīti*, the *bhikkhu* dwells in equanimity [*upekkhā*], with *sati*, fully aware [*sampajāno*], and experiences *sukha* with the body; he enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones declare: ‘One who is equanimous [*upekkhako*], with *sati*, dwells in *sukha*.’
mn27:21.3: ‘the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:22.1: Again, brahmin, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and grief, the *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* [*adukkhamasukhaṁ*], purified by equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*].
mn27:22.2: This too is called, brahmin, a footprint of the Tathāgata, and also something frequented by the Tathāgata, and also something marked by the Tathāgata.
mn27:22.3: But the noble disciple does not yet come to a conclusion:
mn27:22.4: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:23.1: With the mind [*citta*] thus in *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliable, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he directs the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of recollecting past lives.
mn27:23.2: He recollects many kinds of past lives, that is: one birth, two births … thus with features and details he recollects many kinds of past lives.
mn27:23.3: This too is called, brahmin, a footprint of the Tathāgata, and also something frequented by the Tathāgata, and also something marked by the Tathāgata.
mn27:23.4: But the noble disciple does not yet come to a conclusion:
mn27:23.5: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:24.1: With the mind [*citta*] thus in *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliable, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he directs the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the passing away and reappearance of beings.
mn27:24.2: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human … he understands beings passing away and reappearing according to their *kamma*.
mn27:24.3: This too is called, brahmin, a footprint of the Tathāgata, and also something frequented by the Tathāgata, and also something marked by the Tathāgata.
mn27:24.4: But the noble disciple does not yet come to a conclusion:
mn27:24.5: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:25.1: With the mind [*citta*] thus in *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliable, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he directs the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the destruction of the *āsavas*.
mn27:25.2: He understands as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn27:25.3: He understands as it truly is: ‘These are the *āsavas*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of the *āsavas*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of the *āsavas*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of the *āsavas*.’
mn27:25.4: This too is called, brahmin, a footprint of the Tathāgata, and also something frequented by the Tathāgata, and also something marked by the Tathāgata.
mn27:25.5: The noble disciple has not yet reached a conclusion, but now he does come to a conclusion:
mn27:25.6: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:26.1: For one knowing thus and seeing thus, the mind [*citta*] is liberated even from the *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavā*], the mind [*citta*] is liberated even from the *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavā*], the mind [*citta*] is liberated even from the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavā*].
mn27:26.2: When it is liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn27:26.3: He understands: ‘Birth is destroyed, the spiritual life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn27:26.4: This too is called, brahmin, a footprint of the Tathāgata, and also something frequented by the Tathāgata, and also something marked by the Tathāgata.
mn27:26.5: To this extent, brahmin, the noble disciple has reached a conclusion:
mn27:26.6: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:26.7: To this extent, brahmin, the simile of the elephant’s footprint is complete in detail.”
mn27:27.1: When this was said, Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin said to the *Bhagavā*:
mn27:27.3: Just as if one were to set upright what had been overturned, or reveal what had been concealed, or point out the way to one who was lost, or hold up an oil lamp in the darkness so that those with eyes could see forms, so too Master Gotama has made the *dhamma* clear in many ways.
mn27:27.4: I go for refuge to Master Gotama, and to the *dhamma*, and to the *bhikkhu-saṅgha*.
mn27:27.5: Let Master Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forth for life.”
mn27:27.6: The Shorter Simile of the Elephant’s Footprint is finished, the seventh.
mn27:3.1: “Suppose, sir, a skilled elephant tracker were to enter an elephant forest.
mn27:3.2: There he might see a large elephant footprint, long and broad.
mn27:3.3: He might come to a conclusion:
mn27:3.4: ‘Surely, sir, this is a great bull elephant.’
mn27:3.5: Just so, sir, from the time I saw four footprints in the ascetic Gotama, I came to the conclusion:
mn27:3.6: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:4.10: ‘The ascetic Gotama, sir, has arrived at such-and-such a village or town.’
mn27:4.11: They go to the ascetic Gotama.
mn27:4.12: The ascetic Gotama instructs, encourages, inspires, and gladdens them with a talk on *dhamma*.
mn27:4.13: Having been instructed, encouraged, inspired, and gladdened by the ascetic Gotama with a talk on *dhamma*, they do not so much as ask the ascetic Gotama a question—how then could they refute his statement?
mn27:4.14: Instead, they become disciples of the ascetic Gotama.
mn27:4.15: When I saw this first footprint in the ascetic Gotama, then I came to the conclusion:
mn27:4.16: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:4.2: Here, sir, I see certain wise nobles, astute, trained in the doctrines of others, hair-splitters, going about, one would think, breaking apart viewpoints by means of discernment [*paññā*].
mn27:4.4: ‘The ascetic Gotama, sir, will be arriving at such-and-such a village or town.’
mn27:4.6: ‘We will approach the ascetic Gotama and ask him this question.
mn27:4.7: If, when asked by us, he answers like this, then we will refute his statement in this way.
mn27:4.8: And if, when asked by us, he answers like that, then we will refute his statement in that way.’
mn27:5.1: And again, sir, I see here certain wise brahmins …
mn27:7.1: certain wise ascetics, astute, trained in the doctrines of others, hair-splitters, going about, one would think, breaking apart viewpoints by means of discernment [*paññā*].
mn27:7.10: Having been instructed, encouraged, inspired, and gladdened by the ascetic Gotama with a talk on *dhamma*, they do not so much as ask the ascetic Gotama a question—how then could they refute his statement?
mn27:7.11: Instead, they ask the ascetic Gotama himself for the opportunity to go forth from home into homelessness.
mn27:7.12: The ascetic Gotama gives them the going forth.
mn27:7.13: Having gone forth there, dwelling withdrawn, diligent, ardent, and resolute, before long they realize for themselves with direct knowledge and attain in this very life the unsurpassed culmination of the spiritual life, for the sake of which clansmen rightly go forth from home into homelessness, and they dwell in it.
mn27:7.15: ‘We were almost lost, sir, we nearly perished, sir;
mn27:7.16: for formerly, though we were not ascetics, we claimed: “We are ascetics”; though we were not brahmins, we claimed: “We are brahmins”; though we were not arahants, we claimed: “We are arahants.”
mn27:7.17: But now we are ascetics; now we are brahmins; now we are arahants.’
mn27:7.18: When I saw this fourth footprint in the ascetic Gotama, then I came to the conclusion:
mn27:7.19: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’
mn27:7.20: From the time I saw these four footprints in the ascetic Gotama, I came to the conclusion:
mn27:7.21: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* has been well explained by the *Bhagavā*, the disciple-*saṅgha* of the *Bhagavā* is practicing well.’”
mn27:7.3: ‘The ascetic Gotama, sir, will be arriving at such-and-such a village or town.’
mn27:7.4: They prepare a question: ‘We will approach the ascetic Gotama and ask him this question.
mn27:7.5: If, when asked by us, he answers like this, then we will refute his statement in this way.
mn27:7.6: And if, when asked by us, he answers like that, then we will refute his statement in that way.’
mn27:7.7: They hear: ‘The ascetic Gotama, sir, has arrived at such-and-such a village or town.’
mn27:7.8: They go to the ascetic Gotama.
mn27:7.9: The ascetic Gotama instructs, encourages, inspires, and gladdens them with a talk on *dhamma*.
mn27:8.1: When this was said, Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin got down from his all-white mare-drawn chariot, arranged his upper robe over one shoulder, and raising his joined palms toward the *Bhagavā*, uttered this inspired exclamation three times:
mn27:8.2: “Homage to that *Bhagavā*, the arahant, the perfectly awakened one;
mn27:8.3: homage to that *Bhagavā*, the arahant, the perfectly awakened one;
mn27:8.4: homage to that *Bhagavā*, the arahant, the perfectly awakened one.”
mn27:8.5: “Perhaps someday or other we too might meet with Master Gotama; perhaps there might be some conversation.”
mn27:9.1: Then Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin approached the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*.
mn27:9.2: When this courteous and memorable conversation was finished, he sat down to one side.
mn27:9.3: Seated to one side, Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin told the *Bhagavā* all that had been said in his conversation with the wanderer Pilotika.
mn27:9.4: When this was said, the *Bhagavā* said to Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin:
mn27:9.5: “Brahmin, the simile of the elephant’s footprint is not yet complete in detail to this extent.
mn27:9.6: But brahmin, listen to how the simile of the elephant’s footprint is complete in detail.
mn27:9.7: Listen carefully and attend closely. I will speak.”
mn27:9.8: “Yes, sir,” Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin replied to the *Bhagavā*.
